vestibular peripheral simple.name: vestibular peripheral simple
vestibular peripheral simple.comment: ataxia in gait always  seams proprioceptive, even though later I have chosen vestibular ataxia. If I chose only vestibular ataxia, without clicking on ataxia, it is localized correctly to vestibular system.  Without proprioceptive ataxia, this cannot be localized to cervical spine.

peripheral-all LMN.name: peripheral-all LMN
peripheral-all LMN.comment: With all four limbs involved we can not have L4-S3

L4-S3 (caudal-L7-S3).name: L4-S3 (caudal-L7-S3)
L4-S3 (caudal-L7-S3).comment: T3-L3 is not an option here, even with spinal shock because of the tail involment. L7-S3 is not probable, but only possible, because it nornally doesn't come with decreased extensor tone

vestibular central (vertical nystagmus).name: vestibular central (vertical nystagmus)
vestibular central (vertical nystagmus).comment: paradoxycal is not possible here because head tilt and proprioceptive dificit are on thr same side

diencephalon.name: diencephalon
diencephalon.comment: C1-C5 is not an option here with this consciousness level.

vestibular + one pelvic limb.name: vestibular + one pelvic limb
vestibular + one pelvic limb.comment: For vestibular central we need propruoceptive deficit on both limbs on the affected side (here it is left).  If thoracic left limb in this case has normal proprioception, proprioception on the left pelvic lim is nost likely incidental finding. In case PD on left thoracic limb is not corectlly assessed, we still leave option os centra vestibular being possible.

rostral medulla.name: rostral medulla
rostral medulla.comment: Possible-combination of these two areas, they are not possible as two different diagnosis

facial-peripheral.name: facial-peripheral
facial-peripheral.comment: individual cranial nerves deficits should be listed as peripheral nerves

cauda equina.name: cauda equina
cauda equina.comment: T3-L3 is not an option with decreased withdrawal and no ataxia

seizures+compulsive.name: seizures+compulsive
seizures+compulsive.comment: With seizures and behaviour changes forebrain is definite

MF-seizures+hypermetria+cerebellar ataxi.name: MF-seizures+hypermetria+cerebellar ataxia
MF-seizures+hypermetria+cerebellar ataxi.comment: cerebellar lesion cannot cause forebrain signal (seizures)

MF-seizures+vestibular ataxia+head tilt.name: MF-seizures+vestibular ataxia+head tilt
MF-seizures+vestibular ataxia+head tilt.comment: vestibular signs (vestibular ataxia, head tilt)+ forebrain signs (seizures)= multifocal

MF-seizures+somnolent+vestibular ataxia+.name: MF-seizures+somnolent+vestibular ataxia+head tilt
MF-seizures+somnolent+vestibular ataxia+.comment: vestibular lesion cannot cause consciousness level changes

MF-seizures+cerebelar ataxia.name: MF-seizures+cerebelar ataxia
MF-seizures+cerebelar ataxia.comment: cerebellar lesion cannot cause seizures, for that we need forebrain involvement-multifocal

MF-cerebellar ataxia+compulsion.name: MF-cerebellar ataxia+compulsion
MF-cerebellar ataxia+compulsion.comment: behavioral changes are forebrain sign, in combination with cerebellar signs (cerebellar ataxia), we have multifocal localisation

MF-cerebellar ataxia+consciousness.name: MF-cerebellar ataxia+consciousness
MF-cerebellar ataxia+consciousness.comment: consciousness level is forebrain sign, cerebellar ataxia is cerebellar signs= multifocal

MF-forebrain+vestibular+cerebellar.name: MF-forebrain+vestibular+cerebellar
MF-forebrain+vestibular+cerebellar.comment: forebrain signs (compulsion, seizures)+cerebellar signs (hypermetria, head tremor, cerebellar ataxia)+vestibular signs (head tilt, vestibular ataxia)= multifocal

vestibular and cerebellar ataxia.name: vestibular and cerebellar ataxia
vestibular and cerebellar ataxia.comment: Peripheral vestibular is impossible here, there is clear involvement of cerebellum. I would add multifocal here as possible.

spinal pain all levels.name: spinal pain all levels
spinal pain all levels.comment: we don't have severe pain override in L4-S3 but we should add it.

multifocal crazy.name: multifocal crazy
multifocal crazy.comment: all possibles that were here are actually impossible. here we have forebrain, vestibular  signs, so it is multifocal

cer+for+vest.name: cer+for+vest
cer+for+vest.comment: There are no forebrain signs, we have vestibular and cerebellar signs

vestibular ataxia with hypermetria.name: vestibular ataxia with hypermetria
vestibular ataxia with hypermetria.comment: vestibular peripheral cannot exist with central signs (cerebellar in this case)

brain stem multifocal.name: brain stem multifocal
brain stem multifocal.comment: we have diffuse brain stem plus forebrain signs= multifocal

1.comment: Seizures and abnormal behavior localise to the forebrain. Deficits are on the right side, so the lesion is on the left side. Reduced menace with normal pupillary light reflex supports a lesion in the forebrain, not in the brainstem.

2.comment: : Circling, disorientation, and abnormal behavior suggest forebrain disease. Postural deficits are contralateral to the lesion, so left-sided deficits indicate a right forebrain lesion.

3.comment:
Bilateral forebrain.
Explanation: Seizures and abnormal mentation indicate forebrain disease. Apparent blindness with normal pupillary light reflexes suggests cortical blindness. Because both eyes are affected, the lesion is most likely bilateral forebrain.

4.comment:
Right brainstem, central vestibular system.
Explanation: Vestibular signs can be central or peripheral. Altered mentation and postural deficits in all four limbs indicate a central lesion. Ipsilateral facial nerve dysfunction makes a right brainstem lesion most likely.

5.comment:
Caudal brainstem, especially medulla.
Explanation: Multiple cranial nerve deficits together with tetraparesis localise to the brainstem. Involvement of gag and tongue function suggests the caudal brainstem.

6.comment: Hypermetria, intention tremor, and broad-based stance are classic cerebellar signs. Absent menace with normal vision and normal pupillary light reflex is also typical for cerebellar disease.

7.comment:
Left cerebellum or left cerebellar peduncle, paradoxical vestibular syndrome.
Explanation: In paradoxical vestibular disease, the head tilt is away from the lesion. The cerebellar signs on the left indicate the lesion is on the left side.

8.comment:
Multifocal central nervous system disease.
Explanation: Seizures suggest forebrain disease. Vestibular signs and tetraparesis suggest brainstem involvement. Neck pain also suggests additional disease. One single lesion does not explain all findings well.

10.comment:
Right C1 to C5 spinal cord.
Explanation: The lesion affects both right limbs but not cranial nerves. Reflexes are not reduced, so this is an upper motor neuron lesion in the cervical spinal cord.

13.comment:
Left C6 to T2 nerve roots or brachial intumescence.
Explanation: This pattern suggests a lesion affecting the lower motor neurons to the thoracic limb. Neck pain and one-limb involvement suggest nerve root disease or a root signature.

17.comment: Schiff-Sherrington posture-It can occur with severe T3 to L3 lesions and does not mean the thoracic limb lower motor neurons are damaged.

19.comment:
Cauda equina or lumbosacral nerve roots.
Explanation: Pain over the lumbosacral area, tail weakness, and urinary problems strongly suggest cauda equina disease rather than a more cranial spinal cord lesion. Normal patellar reflexes ad weaker withdrawal.

20.comment:
Sacral segments or sacral peripheral nerves.
Explanation: The main deficits involve tail and perineal function. That localises to the sacral spinal cord segments or their peripheral nerves.

22.comment:
Right peripheral vestibular system, likely middle or inner ear region.
Explanation: CN VII dysfunction, vestibular signs, and Horner syndrome can all occur together with disease in the middle or inner ear region. Normal mentation and normal postural reactions support a peripheral lesion

23.comment:
Bilateral trigeminal nerve, mainly motor component.
Explanation: The muscles of mastication are innervated by the trigeminal nerve. A dropped jaw and bilateral masticatory muscle atrophy strongly support bilateral trigeminal motor dysfunction.

24.comment:
Left facial nerve, peripheral.
Explanation: Facial asymmetry and poor blink with normal facial sensation indicate facial nerve dysfunction, not trigeminal dysfunction.

27.comment:
Right hypoglossal nerve.
Explanation: Tongue deviation occurs toward the side of the lesion because the normal side pushes the tongue away in chronic cases.  In the acute phase tongue deviates away from the lesion, normal side will have normal tone and affected decreased tone.

28.comment:
Glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves, or their nuclei in the medulla.
Explanation: Gag, swallowing, and laryngeal function depend mainly on CN IX and CN X. If there are no long tract deficits, a peripheral lower cranial nerve lesion is possible.

33.comment:
Left peripheral vestibular system with left facial nerve involvement.
Explanation: This pattern fits a lesion in the petrous temporal or middle ear region affecting CN VII and CN VIII.

35.comment:
Right brachial plexus avulsion, especially C8 to T2 roots.
Explanation: Severe lower motor neuron deficits in the thoracic limb plus Horner syndrome strongly suggest brachial plexus avulsion with root involvement.

41.comment:
: Left sciatic nerve.
Explanation: A normal patellar reflex means the femoral nerve is functioning. Reduced withdrawal and distal limb sensory loss fit sciatic nerve dysfunction.

43.comment:
Tibial nerve.
Explanation: The tibial nerve supplies the extensor muscles of the hock and sensation to the plantar surface.

45.comment:
Generalized peripheral neuropathy or diffuse lower motor neuron disease.
Explanation: Generalized weakness with reduced reflexes and neurogenic atrophy indicates a diffuse lower motor neuron disorder.

46.comment:
Neuromuscular junction.
Explanation: Exercise-induced weakness and fatigability are classic for neuromuscular junction disease. Regurgitation suggests possible megaesophagus.

48.comment:
Diffuse lower motor neuron disease, likely peripheral nerve or neuromuscular junction (less likely but possible pre-synaptic).
Explanation: This is a generalized flaccid paralysis pattern. The lesion is peripheral, not central, because mentation is normal and reflexes are reduced.

49.comment:
: Generalized muscle disease or neuromuscular junction disease.
Explanation: Ventroflexion is common with neuropathy, myopathy and some neuromuscular junction disorders. It is possible with cervical myelopahies but than we have UMN pelvic limbs and no generalised weakness

50.comment:
Generalized peripheral motor nerve roots and nerves, polyradiculoneuritis pattern.
Explanation: Acute flaccid weakness with reduced reflexes and normal mentation strongly supports a generalized lower motor neuron disorder affecting nerve roots and peripheral nerves.

C27.name: C27
C27.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Lumbosacral stenosis / cauda equina compression, affecting
L7 and sacral nerve roots (S1-S3) — patellar reflex integrity confirms L4-L6
are spared

[EXPLANATION]
The patellar reflex is the most important discriminating finding:
NORMAL patellar reflex confirms L4-L6 (femoral nerve) are INTACT.
This places the lesion CAUDAL to L6, at L7-S3.
If the lesion were at T3-L3: UMN signs (increased reflexes, spasticity,
postural deficits) would be expected — not LMN pattern.
If the lesion involved L4-L6: patellar reflex would be reduced or absent.
Signs and their nerve root attribution:
  Tail hypotonia: caudal nerve roots (Cd1-5) and S3 — AFFECTED ✓
  Perineal hypoalgesia: S1-S3 dermatome — AFFECTED ✓
  Reduced anal reflex: pudendal nerve (S1-S3) — AFFECTED ✓
  Reduced hock flexion in withdrawal: sciatic nerve, specifically S1
    component — AFFECTED ✓ (L6-S1 compose the sciatic nerve; S1 involvement
    explains the withdrawal deficit while L6 may be relatively spared)
  Atonic bladder (LMN): pelvic nerve (S1-S3) — detrusor innervation lost ✓
  Patellar reflex NORMAL: L4-L6 (femoral nerve) — INTACT ✓

C5.name: C5
C5.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Left second-order (preganglionic) neuron Horner's syndrome —
sympathetic trunk at the thoracic inlet or T1-T3 nerve root level

[EXPLANATION]
Horner's syndrome localisation requires identifying which neuron in the
three-neuron sympathetic arc is affected:
  First-order neuron (hypothalamus → T1-T3 spinal cord):
    Associated with ipsilateral hemiplegia, brainstem signs, or cervical
    myelopathy signs. Inthis case ruled out: normal gait and postural reactions.
  Second-order neuron / preganglionic (T1-T3 → cranial cervical ganglion,
    coursing over the thoracic inlet and up the neck):
    Associated with thoracic or brachial plexus pathology. Isolated Horner's
    with chest signs is the possible  presentation. FITS HERE.
  Third-order neuron / postganglionic (cranial cervical ganglion → orbit,
    traveling with internal carotid and ophthalmic nerve):
    Produces isolated Horner's, no other neurological signs, often idiopathic
    in cats. Does not explain the coughing .
Left coughing + left Horner's = thoracic or
mediastinal mass compressing the preganglionic sympathetic trunk as it
courses over the thoracic inlet. Of course, coughing is a soft finding, regrdless of that, localisation is peripheral.

C6.name: C6
C6.comment:
All deficits are attributable to bilateral CN VII motor failure.
CRITICAL TEACHING POINT — Absent menace does NOT indicate blindness here:
The menace response requires an intact efferent limb (CN VII → blink).
If CN VII is bilaterally non-functional, the animal CANNOT blink regardless
of visual input. The menace will be absent despite intact vision.
Evidence that vision is intact:
- Normal PLR bilaterally (CN II afferent and CN III efferent are intact)
- Normal visual obstacle course (conscious vision present)
The absent menace is entirely explained by bilateral CN VII efferent failure —
it is not an indicator of a forebrain or optic pathway lesion in this case.
Normal gait and postural reactions confirm no brainstem or forebrain
involvement, which rules out a central CN VII nucleus lesion in the pons.
For a central CN VII lesion to explain bilateral facial paresis, a bilateral
caudal pontine lesion would be required — which would inevitably produce
additional brainstem signs (postural deficits, altered mentation, other
CN deficits).

C7.name: C7
C7.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Peripheral CN IX (glossopharyngeal) and CN X (vagus) neuropathy,
at or distal to the jugular foramen; vagal neuropathy cannot be distinguished
from nucleus ambiguus involvement (medulla) without imaging

[EXPLANATION]
CN IX (glossopharyngeal): innervates pharyngeal muscles (stylopharyngeus),
provides pharyngeal sensation, and contributes the afferent limb of the
gag reflex.
CN X (vagus): innervates pharyngeal and laryngeal muscles via the nucleus
ambiguus, provides the efferent limb of the gag reflex, and its recurrent
laryngeal branch innervates the intrinsic laryngeal muscles (voice, stridor).
Absent gag reflex: CN IX afferent or CN X efferent failure (or both).
Dysphagia + regurgitation: pharyngeal motor failure (CN X, nucleus ambiguus).
Dysphonia + stridor: laryngeal paresis/paralysis (recurrent laryngeal nerve,
branch of CN X).
Normal tongue (CN XII): hypoglossal nucleus and nerve intact. This rules out
a caudal medullary lesion affecting both CN X and CN XII nuclei (which are
adjacent in the caudal medulla).
Normal facial motor (CN VII): pons and CN VII intact, rules out more rostral
brainstem involvement.
Normal gait and postural reactions: no long tract involvement, favoring
peripheral nerve over nuclear (nucleus ambiguus) origin — though imaging is
required to definitively distinguish peripheral CN IX/X from a medullary
lesion.
If postural deficits or other brainstem signs were present,
the localisation would shift to medulla-caudal (nucleus ambiguus).

C8.name: C8
C8.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Right lateral medulla (rostral medulla) —

[EXPLANATION]
Head tilt and falling to the right: vestibular system affected on the right.
Rotary nystagmus: can occur in both central and peripheral vestibular disease
— not discriminating alone in this case.
Key finding: IPSILATERAL postural reaction deficits (right-sided deficits
with right-sided vestibular signs).
In peripheral vestibular disease, postural reactions are NORMAL because
the lesion does not affect central pathways.
Postural reaction deficits confirm a CENTRAL (brainstem) lesion.
The combination of ipsilateral vestibular signs + ipsilateral postural deficits
localises to the MEDULLA at or above the level where ascending proprioceptive
tracts are still ipsilateral.
Right-sided Horner's syndrome: the descending sympathetic tract runs through
the LATERAL medulla on the same side as the body it serves (ipsilateral).
A right lateral medullary lesion interrupts the right descending sympathetic
tract → right Horner's. This is a key anatomical landmark.
Normal CN VII bilaterally: CN VII nucleus is in the caudal PONS, not the
medulla. Its integrity places the lesion caudal to the pons (medulla) or
confirms the pons is spared.
This is the lateral medullary syndrome :
ipsilateral vestibular signs + ipsilateral Horner's + ipsilateral postural
deficits from a single lateral medullary lesion.

C9.name: C9
C9.comment:
Paradoxical vestibular syndrome occurs when a cerebellar lesion disrupts
vestibular modulation, causing vestibular signs that point to the OPPOSITE
side from the actual lesion.
Mechanism:
The flocculonodular lobe of the cerebellum provides inhibitory input to the
IPSILATERAL vestibular nuclei. A LEFT cerebellar lesion removes inhibition
from the LEFT vestibular nuclei → left vestibular nuclei become overactive
→ clinical signs mimic a RIGHT peripheral vestibular lesion.
Therefore: head tilt, falling, and rolling to the LEFT appear to indicate a
left-sided vestibular lesion — but this is PARADOXICAL because the actual
lesion is RIGHT cerebellar, not RIGHT vestibular.
How to identify the paradox:
- Postural reaction deficits on the RIGHT: cerebellar hemisphere lesions
  cause ipsilateral limb deficits (cerebellum does not decussate for limb
  coordination). Right-sided deficits = RIGHT-sided cerebellar or central
  pathway lesion — but the vestibular signs point LEFT. This discordance
  reveals the paradox.
SIMPLE RULE TO CALL PARADOXICAL – LESION IN ON THE SAME SIDE OF POSTURAL REACTION DEFICITS OR HYPERMETRIA. REMEMBER THIS – LEGS DON’T LIE!

C11-normal.name: C11-normal
C11-normal.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Right cortex / visual cortex (with possible bilateral
or contralateral extension given bilateral visual deficits)

[EXPLANATION]
Normal PLR + absent menace + normal fundic exam + absent visual placing =
CORTICAL BLINDNESS.
The PLR pathway: retina → optic nerve → optic chiasm → optic tract →
pretectal area → CN III nucleus. This pathway does NOT require cortical
processing. Normal PLRs confirm CN II (retina to midbrain) and CN III are
intact.
The menace and visual placing pathways require: CN II → lateral geniculate
nucleus → optic radiations → visual cortex (occipital lobe) but then it goes to the motor cortex. If the visual
cortex or optic radiations are damaged, menace and visual placing are lost
despite normal PLRs.
Conclusion: the lesion is in the visual cortex (occipital lobe)/motor cortex or optic
radiations — NOT in the retina or optic nerves.
Left postural reaction deficits: right hemisphere lesion (contralateral
control). This confirms a right-sided forebrain but then in the motor cortex lesion.
Right-sided focal seizures (facial twitching): cortical irritation of the
LEFT motor cortex (contralateral to body side). This implies left hemisphere
involvement OR bilateral disease.
Bilateral visual deficits from forebrain disease: in dogs and cats, visual
cortical representation is partly bilateral (unlike humans). A large right
occipital lesion can produce bilateral visual field deficits. Alternatively,
bilateral occipital lesions explain bilateral blindness more cleanly.
ALWAYS consider cortical blindness when PLRs are normal
and menace/placing are absent. Do not diagnose retinal or optic nerve disease
without ruling out a forebrain lesion.

C12.name: C12
C12.comment:
Circling and head turn toward the LEFT: in forebrain/thalamic disease,
animals circle toward the side of the lesion. Left circling = left-sided lesion.
However: forebrain hemisphere lesions produce CONTRALATERAL postural
reaction deficits (right hemisphere → left limb deficits; left hemisphere →
right limb deficits). This is a key internal consistency check.
Altered mentation (obtunded): forebrain (cerebral cortex or thalamus)
or ascending reticular activating system (ARAS) in the brainstem.
No nystagmus distinguishes this from vestibular disease, which can
superficially mimic forebrain signs (circling, head turn).
Normal menace and PLR: primary visual pathways intact, no occipital cortex
or optic pathway failure.
Thalamus (diencephalon) is favoured over cortex alone because:
- Obtundation is more prominent with thalamic lesions (thalamus is integral
  to the ARAS and consciousness)

C16.name: C16
C16.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Peripheral nerve and muscle ischemia secondary to aortic
thromboembolism (ATE, saddle thrombus)

WARNING: THIS IS NOT A PRIMARY NEUROLOGICAL LESION. This is vascular
disease causing peripheral nerve and muscle ischemia. Assigning to
'peripheral' reflects the site of neurological dysfunction (ischemic
peripheral nerves and muscles), but the primary diagnosis is vascular.
Do NOT localise this to the spinal cord.

[EXPLANATION]
ATE occurs when a thrombus (commonly from cardiomyopathy) lodges at the
aortic trifurcation, obstructing blood flow to both pelvic limbs.
This mimics T3-L3 myelopathy or lumbosacral myelopathy clinically but
is NOT a spinal cord lesion.
Critical differentiating features from myelopathy:
- ABSENT bilateral femoral pulses: NEVER absent in myelopathy. This is
  the single most important differentiating sign.
- COLD pelvic limbs: ischemia causes peripheral vasoconstriction and
  absent perfusion. Myelopathy does not affect limb temperature.
- FIRM, PAINFUL gastrocnemius muscles: ischemic myopathy and muscle
  contracture. Acute myelopathy causes flaccid, non-painful muscles.
- HYPERACUTE simultaneous bilateral onset: ATE strikes both limbs
  simultaneously and instantaneously. Myelopathy can be acute but rarely
  perfectly bilateral from onset.
- Elevated CK and lactate (if measured): ischemic muscle damage.
The pelvic limb paralysis and absent reflexes/pain are due to peripheral
nerve ischemia, not spinal cord damage. The nerve and muscle fail because
they are not perfused — not because the spinal cord is damaged.
Bladder distension is shared with both conditions and does not discriminate.

C17.name: C17
C17.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Left sciatic nerve injury, complete (both common peroneal
and tibial branches affected), distal to the hamstring branches (proximal
sciatic nerve intact)

[EXPLANATION]
Sciatic nerve (L6-S1) divides into two major branches:
  Common peroneal nerve: hock flexion (dorsiflexion), digit extension,
    sensory to dorsal paw.
  Tibial nerve: hock extension (plantarflexion), digital flexion, sensory
    to plantar paw surface.
ABSENT hock flexion: common peroneal nerve affected.
WEAK hock extension: tibial nerve affected.
ABSENT deep pain plantar digits: tibial nerve sensory affected.
Sciatic distribution hypalgesia: confirms sciatic territory.
Cannot bear weight on digits: combined peroneal + tibial failure.
NORMAL stifle flexion and hip flexion: the hamstring muscles (biceps
femoris, semimembranosus, semitendinosus) are innervated by PROXIMAL
branches of the sciatic nerve. Their function confirms the lesion is
DISTAL to the hamstring branches of the sciatic nerve.
This is critical: if stifle flexion were absent, the lesion would be
more proximal (at or near the sciatic notch or higher).
NORMAL patellar reflex (not explicitly tested but implied by normal
stifle behavior): confirms femoral nerve (L4-L6) is intact — rules out
a lesion at the level of the lumbar plexus.
Differential nerve localisation:
- Isolated peroneal: hock flexion lost, dorsal sensation lost, but plantar
  sensation and hock extension intact.
- Isolated tibial: hock extension weak, plantar sensation lost, but hock
  flexion and dorsal sensation intact.
- Complete sciatic (distal to hamstrings): all of the above affected — fits
  this case.

C20 without vestibular.name: C20 without vestibular
C20 without vestibular.comment:
SUBDIVISION: At minimum two lesions — (1) left forebrain/cerebral cortex
(right focal seizures, right postural deficits) AND (2) right caudal pons
or peripheral right CN VII (right facial paresis).

C20 with vestibular.name: C20 with vestibular
C20 with vestibular.comment:
SUBDIVISION: At minimum two lesions — (1) left forebrain/cerebral cortex
(right focal seizures, right postural deficits) AND (2) right caudal pons
or peripheral right CN VII (right facial paresis). Prior vestibular episode
suggests a third area (vestibular system) was previously involved.

[EXPLANATION]
Focal right-sided facial twitching seizures: irritative focus in the LEFT
motor cortex (cortical seizures are contralateral to the body side affected).
Right postural reaction deficits: LEFT forebrain/UMN pathway. Confirms
left hemisphere lesion.
Right facial paresis (CN VII): RIGHT-sided CN VII lesion — either at the
right CN VII nucleus (caudal pons) or the right peripheral facial nerve.
This is CONTRALATERAL to the forebrain lesion (which is left-sided).
Therefore: the right facial paresis CANNOT be explained by the same left
forebrain lesion.
Absent right menace: could be due to right CN VII failure (efferent limb
of menace absent → no blink) OR left visual cortex involvement. In the
context of existing right CN VII paresis, the absent right menace is
explained by the CN VII efferent failure and does not necessarily indicate
a second visual cortex lesion.
Prior vestibular episode (resolved): indicates previous involvement of a
vestibular-associated area (inner ear, vestibular nuclei, or cerebellum) —
this adds a third potential lesion site or indicates a multifocal process
that has evolved.
Conclusion: minimum two active lesions (left forebrain + right CN VII/pons).

C21.name: C21
C21.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Neuromuscular junction (NMJ) — consistent with myasthenia
gravis (acquired or congenital)

[EXPLANATION]
The hallmark finding is FATIGABILITY: weakness that worsens with exercise
or repetitive use and improves with rest.
This is NOT a feature of:
- Neuropathy: produces consistent deficits that do not fluctuate with
  exercise in this pattern.
- Myopathy: weakness is consistent; fatigability specific to exercise
  is not characteristic.
- UMN or LMN spinal cord disease: produces static or progressive deficits,
  not episodic fatigue-dependent weakness.
Fatigability is the physiological signature of NMJ disease: each nerve
impulse releases acetylcholine; if acetylcholine receptors are blocked
(as in MG) or depleted, repeated stimulation leads to progressive failure
of neuromuscular transmission → increasing weakness.
Megaesophagus: the canine esophagus is striated muscle innervated by
vagal motor fibers via NMJ synapses. MG-related NMJ failure at esophageal
junctions → esophageal hypomotility → megaesophagus and regurgitation.
This is one of the most common and important presentations of MG in dogs.
Post-exercise facial paresis: CN VII motor (facial muscles have NMJs).
With insufficient acetylcholine receptor function, repeated facial muscle
activation during exercise leads to NMJ failure → transient facial paresis.
No muscle atrophy: no denervation, no primary muscle destruction (no
axonal loss, no myopathy).
Reduced reflexes: NMJ failure can impair the motor efferent component of
reflex arcs, producing mildly reduced reflexes.
Localisation is at the NEUROMUSCULAR JUNCTION — not within the CNS,
peripheral nerve axon, or muscle fiber. The lesion is at the synapse.

C24.name: C24
C24.comment: Plantigrade or palmigrade stance usually indicate peripheral problem

C28.name: C28
C28.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Isolated sacral segment (S1-S3) lesion — patellar reflex
and limb motor function intact confirm L4-S1 motor is spared; the lesion
affects only sacral autonomic and somatic nerve roots

[EXPLANATION]
Normal gait and postural reactions: L4-L7 nerve roots that supply pelvic
limb motor function (sciatic via L6-S1, femoral via L4-L6) are functionally
INTACT.
Normal patellar reflex: L4-L6 (femoral nerve) INTACT ✓
Normal withdrawal reflex (all components including hock flexion): sciatic
nerve (L6-S1) motor function INTACT ✓
These normal findings place the motor lesion at the SACRAL level (S1-S3
autonomic and somatic) where sphincter and perineal innervation originates,
but the motor supply to the limbs is spared.
Isolated sacral deficits:
  Atonic bladder (LMN): pelvic nerve (S1-S3) — detrusor innervation lost ✓
  Fecal incontinence: internal and external anal sphincter (S1-S3 pudendal
    nerve) — AFFECTED ✓
  Reduced anal reflex: pudendal nerve (S1-S3) — AFFECTED ✓
  Perineal hypoalgesia: S1-S3 dermatome — AFFECTED ✓
How this differs from an L7-dominated lesion (Case 027):
  L7 lesion (lumbosacral junction): affects L7 nerve root → sciatic motor
    contributions → pelvic limb weakness and reduced withdrawal would be
    expected in addition to sphincter signs.
  Pure sacral lesion: sphincter and perineal signs ONLY — limbs are
    completely normal

C29.name: C29
C29.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Right median nerve injury — isolated sensory and distal motor
deficit in the median nerve distribution (C8-T1)

[EXPLANATION]
This is a pure peripheral nerve sensory localisation challenge.
Median nerve (C8-T1): motor supply to carpal and digital flexors (flexor
carpi radialis, superficial and deep digital flexors); sensory supply to
the medial and palmar surface of the paw and medial antebrachium.
Absent deep pain medial antebrachium and medial/palmar digits: exactly
the sensory distribution of the median nerve ✓
PRESENT pain dorsal and lateral limb: dorsal paw (radial nerve, C7-T1)
and lateral limb (radial and musculocutaneous nerve) are INTACT.
NORMAL biceps reflex: musculocutaneous nerve (C6-C8) — INTACT. Rules out
musculocutaneous nerve lesion and C6-C7 root involvement.
NORMAL triceps reflex: radial nerve (C7-T1) — INTACT. Rules out radial
nerve and C7-T1 root.
NORMAL withdrawal reflex (full flexion): both proximal flexors (biceps/
musculocutaneous) and distal flexors (median and ulnar nerve) participate
in withdrawal. Normal full withdrawal indicates the efferent (motor)
component is largely intact — this lesion may be predominantly sensory,
or the motor loss is mild/partial.
NORMAL postural reactions when placed: radial nerve proprioceptive function
(dorsal digit contact) is intact — the dog can perceive dorsal surface
contact and correct posture.
Why not medial cord of brachial plexus?
  Medial cord → median AND ulnar nerves. Ulnar nerve: sensory to lateral
  palmar surface and lateral digit, motor to carpal and digital flexors
  (ulnar head). Ulnar involvement would add lateral palmar hypoalgesia —
  absent here. Medial cord lesion = combined median + ulnar distribution.
Distal atrophy: consistent with denervation of carpal/digital flexors
(median nerve motor supply).

C30?????????.name: C30?????????
C30?????????.comment:
SUBDIVISION: Left lateral pontine tegmentum (caudal pons) — single lesion
producing crossed signs; left-sided cranial nerve deficits with contralateral
(right) limb UMN signs

[EXPLANATION]
This is the definitive brainstem crossed-signs case. All signs are
explained by ONE left-sided lesion at the level of the caudal pons /
pons-medulla junction. The key principle is that CROSSED SIGNS = BRAINSTEM.

LEFT-SIDED signs (ipsilateral to the lesion — all arising from structures
at the lesion level that serve the ipsilateral side):
  Left head tilt: left vestibular nuclei (located at the pontomedullary
    junction, predominantly pons/medulla) are disrupted ✓
  Nystagmus fast phase to the RIGHT: lesion is LEFT-sided; fast phase
    is directed away from the lesion ✓
  Left Horner's syndrome: descending sympathetic tract runs ipsilaterally
    through the lateral brainstem tegmentum. Disruption at the left pontine/
    medullary level → left Horner's ✓
  Left facial hypoalgesia: spinal nucleus and tract of CN V runs ipsilaterally
    through the lateral brainstem from the pons into the medulla and cervical
    cord. Left lateral pontine/medullary tegmentum lesion → left facial
    pain/temperature loss ✓
  Left facial paresis (CN VII): CN VII nucleus is located in the CAUDAL
    PONS. Left-sided caudal pons lesion → left CN VII motor nucleus or
    fascicles affected → left facial paresis ✓

RIGHT-SIDED signs (contralateral to the lesion — arising from tracts that
serve the contralateral body but have NOT yet decussated at this level):
  Right hemiparesis (UMN): descending corticospinal/rubrospinal tracts run
    in the ventral/lateral pons and have NOT decussated at the level of the
    caudal pons. They decussate at the pyramidal decussation in the caudal
    medulla/cervicomedullary junction. A LEFT pontine lesion interrupts the
    LEFT descending UMN tract, which at this level serves the RIGHT body →
    right hemiparesis ✓
  Left postural deficits: ascending proprioceptive tracts at the pontine
    level are ipsilateral to the body side they serve (they have already
    entered the spinal cord from the left limbs and ascend on the left side
    of the brainstem at this level) → left pontine lesion disrupts left
    ascending proprioceptive input → left postural deficits ✓

Summary of the anatomical logic:
  Ipsilateral at pons level: CN VII nucleus, vestibular nuclei (partly),
    descending sympathetic tract, spinal trigeminal tract, ascending
    proprioceptive tracts.
  Contralateral at pons level: descending UMN tracts (not yet crossed).
Localisation = LEFT caudal pons (pontine tegmentum) because:
  - CN VII nucleus is in caudal pons (explains facial paresis)
  - All other signs are consistent with lateral pontine tegmentum involvement
Rule to teach: if there are ipsilateral cranial nerve signs + contralateral
limb UMN signs, the lesion is in the BRAINSTEM at the level of those
cranial nerve nuclei.
